content.text: What is Windows Hello for Business?

Windows Hello for Business (WHfB) is a phishing-resistant, passwordless sign-in method built into Windows 10 and 11. During enrolment, a public/private key pair is generated inside the device's Trusted Platform Module (TPM). The private key never leaves the TPM. The public key is registered in Entra ID against the user object. On every subsequent sign-in, the user unlocks the TPM with a PIN or biometric — the TPM signs the Entra ID authentication challenge and the user is authenticated without any password or secret being transmitted over the network.

The PIN in WHfB is not a password. It is a local unlock code that never leaves the device — it simply unlocks the TPM key. A six-digit PIN that stays on the device is more secure than a 16-character password that travels over the network.

attrs.alt: Windows Hello for Business — enrolment and Cloud Kerberos Trust sign-in flow

content.text: Deployment Models
WHfB supports three trust models for on-premises resource access. Cloud Kerberos Trust is the recommended choice for new deployments.
Trust Model
Key Storage
On-Prem PKI Required
DC Line-of-Sight at Sign-in
Recommended
Cloud Kerberos Trust
Device TPM
No
No
Yes — new deployments
Key Trust
Device TPM
No
Yes (Kerberos at sign-in)
No — deprecated direction
Certificate Trust
Device TPM + Certificate
Yes (ADCS)
Yes (cert issuance)
Only when PKI already exists
Deploying WHfB via Intune

For Entra-joined and co-managed devices, WHfB is configured through an Intune device configuration profile using the Identity Protection template. This replaces Group Policy for cloud-managed devices.

# Configure Windows Hello for Business via Intune — Identity Protection profile
# This is the modern approach (replaces legacy Group Policy for WHfB)

$token = (Get-AzAccessToken -ResourceUrl "https://graph.microsoft.com").Token
$headers = @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token"; "Content-Type" = "application/json" }

# Create Identity Protection (WHfB) configuration profile
$profile = @{
    "@odata.type"    = "#microsoft.graph.windowsIdentityProtectionConfiguration"
    displayName      = "WHfB Baseline Policy"
    description      = "Windows Hello for Business with Cloud Kerberos Trust"

    # Core WHfB settings
    windowsHelloForBusinessBlocked                 = $false   # must be false to enable WHfB
    useSecurityKeyForSignin                        = $true    # also enable FIDO2 security keys

    # PIN policy
    pinMinimumLength                               = 6
    pinMaximumLength                               = 127
    pinUppercaseCharactersUsage                    = "allowed"
    pinLowercaseCharactersUsage                    = "allowed"
    pinSpecialCharactersUsage                      = "required"  # strengthens PIN
    pinExpirationInDays                            = 0            # 0 = no expiry
    pinPreviousBlockCount                          = 5

    # Biometric
    enhancedBiometricsState                        = "enabled"  # anti-spoofing / IR camera
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 3

$created = Invoke-RestMethod `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/deviceManagement/deviceConfigurations" `
    -Method POST -Headers $headers -Body $profile
Write-Output "Profile created: $($created.id)"

# Assign to all Entra-joined devices via the allDevices target
$assignment = @{
    assignments = @(@{
        target = @{ "@odata.type" = "#microsoft.graph.allDevicesAssignmentTarget" }
    })
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 4

Invoke-RestMethod `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/deviceManagement/deviceConfigurations/$($created.id)/assign" `
    -Method POST -Headers $headers -Body $assignment
Write-Output "Assigned to all devices."
Enabling Cloud Kerberos Trust

Cloud Kerberos Trust requires a special Azure AD Kerberos Server object to be created in the on-premises Active Directory forest. This object allows Entra ID to issue partial Kerberos TGTs that on-premises DCs can validate, giving WHfB users access to Kerberos-protected on-premises resources without requiring a DC connection at sign-in time.

# Enable Cloud Kerberos Trust for WHfB
# This allows WHfB to work for on-premises resource access without key/cert trust infrastructure
# Requires: Entra Connect 2.1+ and Azure AD Kerberos server object in on-prem AD

# Step 1: Install the AzureADHybridAuthenticationManagement PowerShell module
Install-Module -Name AzureADHybridAuthenticationManagement -Force

# Step 2: Connect to Entra ID and on-premises AD
$entraCredential = Get-Credential  # Global Admin or Hybrid Identity Admin
$adCredential    = Get-Credential  # Domain Admin for on-premises AD

# Step 3: Create the Azure AD Kerberos Server object in your AD forest
# This creates a computer object in AD that acts as the Kerberos service
# and registers its keys with Entra ID
Set-AzureADKerberosServer `
    -Domain "contoso.com" `
    -DomainCredential $adCredential `
    -CloudCredential $entraCredential

# Step 4: Verify the Kerberos server was created
Get-AzureADKerberosServer -Domain "contoso.com" -DomainCredential $adCredential `
    -CloudCredential $entraCredential

# Output shows:
#   Id           : <object ID>
#   UserAccount  : CN=krbtgt_AzureAD,CN=Users,DC=contoso,DC=com
#   ComputerAccount: CN=AzureADKerberos,OU=Domain Controllers,DC=contoso,DC=com
#   DomainDnsName: contoso.com
#   KeyVersion   : 1
#   KeyUpdatedOn : <timestamp>

# Step 5: Configure WHfB policy to use Cloud Kerberos Trust (in Intune or Group Policy)
# In Intune (Settings Catalog):
#   Windows Hello for Business > Use Cloud Trust For On Prem Auth = Enabled

# Step 6: Verify Cloud Kerberos TGT is being issued at sign-in
# On a test device, after WHfB sign-in:
klist | Select-String "Cloud Kerberos"
# Should show: Cloud Kerberos TGT in the cache
The Azure AD Kerberos computer object (CN=AzureADKerberos) must be created in every AD forest where users need on-prem resource access
Rotate the Kerberos server keys annually: Update-AzureADKerberosServer
Cloud Kerberos Trust requires Windows 10 21H2 or later (or Windows 11 21H2+)
Entra Connect 2.1+ automatically handles the key rotation synchronisation
Group Policy for Hybrid Environments

For hybrid Entra-joined devices managed through Group Policy rather than Intune, WHfB settings are configured under Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows Hello for Business.

# Group Policy settings for Windows Hello for Business (Hybrid environments)
# For cloud-only / Entra-joined environments, use Intune instead of GPO

# GPO path: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates >
#           Windows Components > Windows Hello for Business

# Required settings for Cloud Kerberos Trust:
#
# Use Windows Hello for Business            = Enabled
# Use certificate for on-premises auth      = Disabled   (key trust / cloud kerberos, not cert trust)
# Use cloud trust for on prem auth          = Enabled    (Cloud Kerberos Trust)
# Use a hardware security device (TPM)      = Enabled (if strict)
# Enable PIN recovery                       = Enabled    (Microsoft PIN Reset Service)

# PIN complexity (Optional — enforce stronger PINs):
# Require digits                            = Enabled
# Minimum PIN length                        = 6
# Maximum PIN length                        = 127
# Require lowercase letters                 = Enabled
# Require uppercase letters                 = Enabled
# Require special characters                = Enabled

# PowerShell: Apply WHfB GPO settings programmatically using Set-GPRegistryValue
Import-Module GroupPolicy

$gpoName = "WHfB - Cloud Kerberos Trust"
$gpo = New-GPO -Name $gpoName -Comment "Windows Hello for Business with Cloud Kerberos Trust"

# Enable WHfB
Set-GPRegistryValue -Name $gpoName `
    -Key "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\PassportForWork" `
    -ValueName "Enabled" -Type DWord -Value 1

# Enable Cloud Kerberos Trust
Set-GPRegistryValue -Name $gpoName `
    -Key "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\PassportForWork\Kerberos" `
    -ValueName "UseCloudTrustForOnPremAuth" -Type DWord -Value 1

# Link to domain
New-GPLink -Name $gpoName -Target "DC=contoso,DC=com" -LinkEnabled Yes
Write-Output "WHfB GPO created and linked."
Auditing WHfB Registration Coverage

Track how many users have successfully enrolled WHfB across the tenant. The Microsoft Graph authentication registration report includes windowsHelloForBusiness in the methodsRegistered list for enrolled users.

# Audit WHfB registration state across the tenant via Microsoft Graph
import requests

def get_token():
    r = requests.post(
        "https://login.microsoftonline.com/YOUR_TENANT_ID/oauth2/v2.0/token",
        data={
            "grant_type": "client_credentials",
            "client_id": "YOUR_APP_ID",
            "client_secret": "YOUR_SECRET",
            "scope": "https://graph.microsoft.com/.default",
        }
    )
    return r.json()["access_token"]

def audit_whfb_registrations(token):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    
    # Registration report shows isPasswordlessCapable and methods registered
    resp = requests.get(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/reports/authenticationMethods/userRegistrationDetails"
        "?$select=userPrincipalName,isMfaRegistered,isPasswordlessCapable,methodsRegistered&$top=999",
        headers=headers
    )
    users = resp.json().get("value", [])
    
    whfb_users = [u for u in users if "windowsHelloForBusiness" in u.get("methodsRegistered", [])]
    not_whfb   = [u for u in users if "windowsHelloForBusiness" not in u.get("methodsRegistered", [])]
    
    print(f"WHfB registered:     {len(whfb_users)}")
    print(f"WHfB not registered: {len(not_whfb)}")
    print(f"Total users:         {len(users)}")
    
    # Find users with devices that should have WHfB but don't
    # Cross-check with device list to find Windows devices without WHfB
    devices_resp = requests.get(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/devices"
        "?$filter=operatingSystem eq 'Windows' and trustType eq 'AzureAd'"
        "&$select=id,displayName,registeredOwners,approximateLastSignInDateTime"
        "&$top=100",
        headers=headers
    )
    entra_joined_windows = devices_resp.json().get("value", [])
    print(f"\nEntra-joined Windows devices: {len(entra_joined_windows)}")
    
    # Devices with no recent sign-in may have stale WHfB config
    from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
    threshold = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(days=90)
    stale = [d for d in entra_joined_windows
             if d.get("approximateLastSignInDateTime") and
             datetime.fromisoformat(d["approximateLastSignInDateTime"].rstrip("Z")).replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc) < threshold]
    print(f"Devices with no sign-in in 90 days: {len(stale)}")
    
    return whfb_users, not_whfb

token = get_token()
audit_whfb_registrations(token)
Troubleshooting WHfB Enrolment

WHfB enrolment failures typically stem from TPM issues, network connectivity problems at provisioning time, or incorrect policy settings. The dsregcmd /status tool and the HelloForBusiness operational event log are the primary diagnostics.

# PowerShell: Diagnose Windows Hello for Business enrolment issues on a device
# Run on the affected Windows device (not remotely)

# 1. Check TPM status
Get-Tpm | Select-Object TpmPresent, TpmReady, TpmEnabled, TpmActivated, TpmOwned

# 2. Check WHfB enrolment state
dsregcmd /status | Select-String -Pattern "NGC|Hello|WHfB|KeySignTest" -Context 0,1

# Interpret dsregcmd output:
#   NgcSet : YES          → WHfB key is provisioned
#   NgcKeyId : ...        → Key ID stored in Azure AD
#   KeySignTest : PASSED  → TPM can sign with the key

# 3. Check event logs for WHfB provisioning errors
$events = Get-WinEvent -LogName "Microsoft-Windows-HelloForBusiness/Operational" `
    -MaxEvents 50 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
$events | Where-Object { $_.Level -le 3 } |  # Warning or Error
    Select-Object TimeCreated, Id, Message |
    Format-List

# Common event IDs:
#   1000 — NGC provisioning: success
#   1001 — NGC provisioning: failed
#   3000 — NGC key registration: success
#   3010 — NGC key registration: failed (check network/Entra ID reachability)

# 4. Force re-enrolment (clears existing WHfB credential and restarts provisioning)
# Warning: this deletes the user's current PIN — they will need to set a new one
$sid = (New-Object System.Security.Principal.NTAccount($env:USERNAME)).Translate(
    [System.Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier]).Value
Remove-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\NGC\$sid" `
    -Recurse -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Write-Output "NGC key cleared — user will be prompted to set up WHfB on next sign-in"

# 5. Verify Entra ID join state
dsregcmd /status | Select-String "AzureADJoined|EnterpriseJoined|DomainJoined"
# Expected for WHfB: AzureADJoined = YES (or HybridJoined = YES)
Symptom
Likely Cause
Fix
dsregcmd shows NgcSet: NO
Provisioning failed or was skipped
Check HelloForBusiness/Operational event log; force re-enrolment
TPM not ready / not present
TPM disabled in BIOS or UEFI
Enable TPM in BIOS; check Secure Boot is on
3010: key registration failed
Device cannot reach Entra ID during provisioning
Verify internet connectivity; check proxy settings for Windows Update endpoints
PIN setup interrupted
Intune policy not applied at provision time
Force Intune sync (MDM sync), then trigger WHfB re-provisioning
Biometric not available
IR camera driver missing
Install Windows Hello compatible camera driver; update via WU
PIN Reset Service

If a user forgets their WHfB PIN, they can reset it at the Windows lock screen using the Microsoft PIN Reset Service — without calling IT. The service authenticates the user via another registered method (Microsoft Authenticator, FIDO2, or email OTP) and then provisions a fresh WHfB key pair. IT administrators must pre-enable two PIN Reset Service applications in the tenant before users can self-serve.

# Microsoft PIN Reset Service -- allow users to reset their WHfB PIN
# without IT intervention (similar to SSPR but for WHfB PIN)

# Step 1: Enable the Microsoft PIN Reset Service
# Two enterprise applications must be consented to in your tenant:
#   Microsoft Pin Reset Service Production: b8456c59-1230-41be-a9f2-9f4fd65f2a6b
#   Microsoft Pin Reset Client Production:  fc0f3af4-6835-4174-b806-f7db311fd2f3

$tenantId = 'YOUR_TENANT_ID'
Write-Output 'Grant consent at:'
Write-Output "https://login.microsoftonline.com/$tenantId/oauth2/authorize?client_id=b8456c59-1230-41be-a9f2-9f4fd65f2a6b&response_type=code&redirect_uri=https://www.microsoft.com"

# Step 2: Enable PIN recovery in Intune
# Account Protection profile > Enable PIN recovery = Yes

# Step 3: Verify on device
# Settings > Accounts > Sign-in options > Collect logs

# When a user forgets their PIN:
# 1. On the lock screen, click 'I forgot my PIN'
# 2. Authenticate with Microsoft Authenticator or another MFA method
# 3. Set a new PIN -- new key pair is provisioned in the TPM
# The old TPM key is deleted and a new one registered in Entra ID

# Clear a user's NGC key to force re-enrolment (admin action on device)
$sid = (New-Object System.Security.Principal.NTAccount($env:USERNAME)).Translate(
    [System.Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier]).Value
Remove-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\NGC\$sid" `
    -Recurse -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Write-Output 'NGC key cleared -- user will set up WHfB on next sign-in'
WHfB on Shared Devices

WHfB works on shared devices: each user who signs in gets their own key pair provisioned in the TPM, stored under their Windows SID. The device TPM can hold multiple simultaneous key containers. This is distinct from Shared Device Mode (used for frontline workers and kiosks) where WHfB is disabled and users sign out completely between sessions.

# WHfB on shared devices -- per-user key pairs
# Each user who signs in to the device gets their own WHfB key pair in the TPM

# List all NGC key containers in the TPM for this device
certutil -key -provider 'Microsoft Platform Crypto Provider' 2>$null |
    Select-String 'S-1-5-21'  # each SID = one user's WHfB key

# Check NGC registry store
Get-ChildItem "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\NGC" |
    ForEach-Object {
        $sid = $_.PSChildName
        $user = try {
            ([System.Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier]$sid).Translate(
                [System.Security.Principal.NTAccount]).Value
        } catch { $sid }
        Write-Output $user
        Get-ItemProperty $_.PSPath | Select-Object NgcKeyId, PreferredCredentialProvider
    }

# Kiosk / frontline worker scenario: shared device mode
# In shared device mode (Intune), WHfB is disabled -- users sign out fully
# between sessions. Single-user scenarios use normal WHfB.
# Configure via Intune: Devices > Configuration > Shared multi-user device
# - Account Management: delete accounts on inactivity or storage threshold
TPM 2.0 supports up to 7 simultaneous WHfB key containers — for high-turnover shared devices, earlier keys are evicted
In Shared Device Mode (Intune Frontline Worker): disable WHfB, use FIDO2 security keys instead for individual authentication
Kiosk accounts (single-app kiosk) should not use WHfB; use auto-logon with a restricted kiosk account instead
Enforcing WHfB via Conditional Access

To require WHfB (or FIDO2) specifically — excluding weaker MFA methods — create a custom Authentication Strength containing only the target methods. Assign this strength to a CA policy for admin portals or sensitive applications. Enable in report-only mode first to identify users who would be blocked.
Monitoring WHfB Adoption via Sign-in Logs

Entra ID sign-in logs record the authenticationMethod used at each sign-in. Tracking the fraction of sign-ins using Windows Hello for Business versus password or SMS MFA measures passwordless adoption and identifies device populations that have not yet enrolled.
authenticationMethod in logs
Meaning
Windows Hello for Business
User authenticated with WHfB PIN or biometric
FIDO2 security key
User used a FIDO2 hardware key
Microsoft Authenticator
Push notification or passwordless phone sign-in
Password
Traditional password -- user not yet using WHfB
Mobile phone
SMS OTP -- migrate away from this method
Summary

Windows Hello for Business provides device-bound, phishing-resistant passwordless authentication for Windows 10/11 managed devices. The private key is generated and stored in the device TPM at enrolment — it cannot be extracted, exported, or phished. Cloud Kerberos Trust is the recommended deployment model: it requires no on-premises PKI, no DC line-of-sight at sign-in, and provides full access to both cloud and on-premises Kerberos resources.
WHfB is phishing-resistant: the TPM private key is origin-bound and hardware-protected
Cloud Kerberos Trust is the recommended trust model — no PKI or DC line-of-sight at sign-in
PIN is a local TPM unlock code, not a password — short PINs are secure because they never leave the device
Deploy via Intune Identity Protection profile for Entra-joined devices; GPO for hybrid
Monitor registration via Graph API userRegistrationDetails report
Troubleshoot with dsregcmd /status and the Microsoft-Windows-HelloForBusiness operational log
